persona.name: Henrik Lindqvist
persona.education: M.Sc. Distributed Systems, KTH Royal Institute of Technology. Twelve years operating production systems for enterprise SaaS companies.
long_term_goals: Reduce the time between an internal developer writing a 50-line script and that script running in production from days to minutes
Eliminate per-app Kubernetes manifests for workloads that do not justify them
Build an internal platform that other engineers actually choose to use instead of routing around

occupation.title: Staff Platform Engineer
occupation.organization: Nordkraft Energy (mid-size renewable energy SaaS, ~180 internal developers)
occupation.description: Lead engineer on a four-person platform team that owns the internal compute and deployment story. Responsible for the substrate that other product engineers ship onto: ingress, runtime, secrets, RBAC, observability, on-call. Reports into the head of platform engineering.

persona.communication_style: Calm, structured, evidence-driven. Runs RFC processes inside the company and expects vendors to engage at the same level. Skeptical of demos that work only on a clean cluster.

traits: Optimizes for blast radius and operator ergonomics, not raw performance
Believes most platform pain comes from accidental complexity
Treats developer experience as a measurable engineering problem

big_five_traits.openness: Moderate-high — adopts new tech only after RFC and pilot
big_five_traits.conscientiousness: Very high — keeps runbooks and SLO dashboards up to date personally
big_five_traits.extraversion: Moderate — comfortable presenting at internal forums and conferences
big_five_traits.agreeableness: Moderate — diplomatic with stakeholders, blunt with vendors
big_five_traits.neuroticism: Low — long incident experience has made him steady under pressure

likes: Per-tenant resource quotas enforced by the runtime
RBAC primitives that map cleanly onto org structure
dislikes: Runtimes that require a separate control plane to be useful
Observability that requires bolting on a sidecar

beliefs: A platform must be visibly cheaper to use than to route around, or developers will route around it
Multi-tenant isolation is a runtime property, not a deployment-time afterthought
Observability that is not on by default is observability that does not exist

projects.title: Nordkraft Internal Compute Platform
projects.description: An internal self-serve compute platform for product teams. Currently a thin layer over Kubernetes with custom CRDs, an opinionated CI pipeline, and a Backstage portal. Pain points: small services pay full k8s overhead, per-tenant SQLite/KV needs are met with hand-rolled glue, on-call burden is rising.
key_contributions: Designed the multi-tenant secrets layer using Vault KV v2
Wrote the internal RFC that established the platform team's charter and SLOs

general: Reads incoming runtime CVEs the day they drop
Tracks platform adoption as a percentage of internal services running on it
Pilots new infrastructure on a small canary tenant before any rollout

morning: Reviews the previous night's platform alerts and SLO burn
Checks the platform team standup notes and the queue of inbound platform requests
workday: Spends two-hour blocks on platform RFCs and design reviews
Pairs with product engineers on platform onboarding
Runs weekly office hours for tenant teams
evening: Reads CNCF and OpenTelemetry mailing lists
Drafts internal blog posts about platform decisions
weekend: Reads infrastructure papers and writes longform notes for the platform team's internal wiki

relationships.name: Sara Thune
relationships.description: Head of platform engineering. Trusts Henrik with technical direction; expects him to defend platform decisions at the architecture review board.

relationships.name: Platform team (3 engineers)
relationships.description: Two platform engineers and one SRE who share on-call rotation with him.

other_facts: current_stack: Self-managed Kubernetes with custom CRDs, ArgoCD, Vault KV v2, Backstage portal, OpenTelemetry collector cluster, Prometheus + Loki + Tempo.
current_stack: Internal developers ship via a thin paved-road CI pipeline that templates a Helm chart, a Dockerfile, and a Backstage entry per service.
jobs_to_be_done: Run hundreds of small internal services on a thin compute substrate without paying per-service Kubernetes overhead.
jobs_to_be_done: Enforce per-tenant CPU, storage, KV, and SQL quotas without writing custom admission controllers.
jobs_to_be_done: Ship OpenTelemetry traces and metrics from every workload by default, with no per-app integration code.
pains: Every new internal service costs the platform team Kubernetes onboarding, secrets wiring, observability glue, and on-call coverage.
pains: Per-tenant isolation today is achieved through namespaces and admission policies that drift from org structure.
pains: Developers route around the platform when the onboarding cost exceeds the value of using it.
adoption_yes_if: freq-cloud can run as a multi-tenant runtime under his existing Kubernetes via Helm without giving up RBAC, quotas, or OTel.
adoption_yes_if: Per-tenant SQLite, KV, secrets, and quotas are runtime primitives, not bolted on per service.
adoption_yes_if: He can layer his existing identity provider on top of the runtime's RBAC model.
adoption_yes_if: OpenTelemetry export is on by default and routable to his existing collector.
rejection_no_if: He would have to rebuild his entire observability and identity stack to fit the runtime's assumptions.
rejection_no_if: The runtime cannot demonstrate stable behavior under realistic per-tenant isolation tests with hundreds of tenants.
rejection_no_if: Operating it requires a separate dedicated team or a vendor SaaS to be viable.
anti_goals: He does not want freq-cloud to replace Kubernetes wholesale; he wants it as a tier above Kubernetes for the long tail of small services.
anti_goals: He does not care about cost optimization for indie users.
anti_goals: He does not want a managed control plane provided by the freq-cloud project.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'paved road', 'platform engineering', 'internal developer platform', 'self-serve compute', 'long tail of services', 'we don't want a pod per script'.
recognition_cues: Asks about per-tenant quota enforcement, RBAC mapping to existing identity providers, and OTel export defaults.
recognition_cues: Frames adoption decisions in terms of operator burden and SLOs, not raw features.
